## Images Field Requirement

The images field in the build config file specifies one or more Linux Docker images to be pushed by Cloud Build to Artifact Registry. If a specified image is not produced during the build, the build will fail.

## Provenance and Private Pools

By default, Cloud Build does not generate Binary Authorization attestations for builds in private pools. However, you can enable attestations for builds in private pools by adding an option to your build file. Specifically, after adding the requestedVerifyOption, Cloud Build enables attestation generation and provenance metadata for your image.

To enable provenance generation for private pools, in the options section of your build config, add the requestedVerifyOption option and set to the value VERIFIED. This setting enables provenance generation and configures Cloud Build to verify that provenance metadata is present.

## Private Pool Limitations

The VPC connection allows you to access the resources in your VPC, but it doesn't allow you to access (peered) managed services such as Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) or Cloud SQL. This is a key limitation when using private pools with transitive peering.

Here is a comprehensive summary of the search results regarding **Google Cloud Build SLSA provenance** and the `requestedVerifyOption: VERIFIED` setting:

---

## Google Cloud Build: SLSA Provenance & `requestedVerifyOption: VERIFIED`

### Overview

Cloud Build supports the generation of build provenance that meets Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts (SLSA) Level 3 assurance, based on the specifications for SLSA version 0.1 and 1.0.

Build provenance is verifiable metadata that you can use to audit builds. Cloud Build can generate provenance aligned with the SLSA v1.0 spec when you use the option `requestedVerifyOption` with triggered builds.

---

### How to Enable: `requestedVerifyOption: VERIFIED`

In the `options` section of your build config, add the `requestedVerifyOption` option and set it to the value `VERIFIED`. This setting enables provenance generation and configures Cloud Build to verify that provenance metadata is present.

Builds will only be marked successful if provenance is generated.

**Example `cloudbuild.yaml` configuration:**
```yaml
options:
  requestedVerifyOption: VERIFIED
steps:
  - name: 'gcr.io/cloud-builders/docker'
    args: ['build', '-t', 'us-docker.pkg.dev/${PROJECT_ID}/my-repo/my-app:${SHORT_SHA}', '.']
images:
  - 'us-docker.pkg.dev/${PROJECT_ID}/my-repo/my-app:${SHORT_SHA}'
```
*(Source: [Google Cloud Build Docs](https://docs.cloud.google.com/build/docs/securing-builds/generate-validate-build-provenance) & [Binary Authorization Docs](https://docs.cloud.google.com/binary-authorization/docs/cv-slsa-check))*

---

### Important Constraints

- Cloud Build only generates build provenance for artifacts stored in **Artifact Registry**.
- To get both SLSA v1.0 and v0.1 provenance, you must build using **triggers**. If you start a build manually (via the gcloud CLI), Cloud Build provides only SLSA v0.1 provenance.
- If provenance output is missing, check that Cloud Build was invoked by a build trigger — Cloud Build doesn't produce provenance information when triggered manually.

---

### SLSA v1.0 Support

As part of support for the SLSA v1.0 spec, Cloud Build provides `buildType` details in build provenance. You can use the `buildType` schema to understand the parameterized template used for the build process, including the values that Cloud Build records and the source of those values.

Builds triggered by repository events such as pushes or pull requests will generate the v1.0 provenance.

---

### Why SLSA Level 3?

Cloud Build achieves Level 3 because each build runs in an isolated VM, the provenance is generated by the platform (not user-controlled steps), and it is signed by Google's infrastructure.

Validating SLSA Level 3 provenance helps verify that a build was produced by an expected source and build system and identifies whether any code was injected via an untrusted source or build system. At SLSA Level 3, the provenance is service-generated (by Cloud Build), verifiable for authenticity and integrity, and non-forgeable — meaning a user cannot tamper with the provenance metadata.

---

### Viewing & Validating Provenance

You can access build provenance metadata for containers by using the **Security insights** side panel within the Google Cloud console, or by using the `gcloud` CLI. The Security insights side panel provides a high-level overview of security information for artifacts stored in Artifact Registry.

The open-source `slsa-verifier` CLI tool supports Cloud Build provenance verification for container images. It verifies the signature over the provenance, handles verification key management, and allows end-users to validate that the container image was generated from the trusted Cloud Build builder and trusted source location.

---

### Use with Binary Authorization

The Binary Authorization continuous validation (CV) SLSA check verifies the SLSA-compliant provenance of container images associated with Pods running on GKE clusters. To use this check, you must build images with Cloud Build, which produces SLSA-compliant provenance.

SLSA Level 3 with Cloud Build is one of the most impactful supply chain security improvements you can make on GCP. The platform does most of the heavy lifting — isolated build environments and signed provenance come out of the box. Your job is to verify that provenance, enforce it with Binary Authorization, and make sure nothing gets deployed without going through the pipeline.

---

**Key Reference:** [Generate and validate build provenance | Cloud Build | Google Cloud Docs](https://docs.cloud.google.com/build/docs/securing-builds/generate-validate-build-provenance)

# Cloud Build Provenance and Deployment Security

## Provenance Requirements

Cloud Build automatically generates and signs attestations during the build process. These attestations can be verified through Binary Authorization to ensure only Cloud Build-generated images are deployed.

## Images Field vs Docker Push

There's a critical distinction in how images are stored:

**Images Field (Recommended):** When you push to Artifact Registry using the `images` field in your `docker build` step, Cloud Build generates attestations automatically.

**Docker Push Limitation:** "Cloud Build does not generate attestations when you push images to Artifact Registry using an explicit `docker push` build step." You must use the `images` field approach instead.

## Private Pool Support for Provenance

By default, private pool builds don't generate attestations. However, you can enable this by adding `requestedVerifyOption: VERIFIED` to your build configuration file. This enables both attestation generation and provenance metadata for your image.

## Multi-Pipeline Architecture

An important architectural consideration: "You must use separate build config files for your build pipeline and deployment pipeline. This is because Cloud Build produces attestations only after the build pipeline completes successfully."

## Key Limitation

Cloud Build and Binary Authorization must operate within the same project, though multi-project setups are possible with proper IAM role configuration for the attestor reference.

# Private Pool Builds and Attestations

Based on the documentation provided, here are the key details about private pool support for attestations and provenance:

## Default Behavior
"Cloud Build does not generate attestations when you push images to Artifact Registry using an explicit `docker push` build step."

By default, Cloud Build skips attestation generation for private pool builds entirely.

## Enabling Attestations in Private Pools
To generate attestations for private pool builds, you must add a configuration option to your build file:

"add the `requestedVerifyOption: VERIFIED` option to your build configuration file"

Here's the relevant code snippet from the documentation:

```yaml
options:
  requestedVerifyOption: VERIFIED
```

## Provenance Metadata
According to the guide: "After adding the `requestedVerifyOption`, Cloud Build enables attestation generation and provenance metadata for your image."

This means that enabling the verify option activates both attestation generation and provenance metadata simultaneously for private pool builds, bringing them to feature parity with standard Cloud Build deployments regarding Binary Authorization compliance.

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding the `--requested-verify-option` flag in Cloud Build:

## Overview

The `requestedVerifyOption` setting in your build config enables and verifies the generation of attestations and provenance metadata for your build. Once set, your builds will only be marked SUCCESS if attestations and provenance are generated.

## Usage

In the options section of your build config, add the requestedVerifyOption option and set to the value VERIFIED. This setting enables provenance generation and configures Cloud Build to verify that provenance metadata is present.

## Possible Values

Requested verifiability options have possible values of: NOT_VERIFIED, VERIFIED.

## Alternative Mechanism - Build Triggers vs. Manual Builds

To get both SLSA v1.0 and v0.1 provenance, you must build using triggers. If you start a build manually, by using the gcloud CLI, Cloud Build provides only SLSA v0.1 provenance.

## Viewing Provenance

To view provenance metadata for container images, you can run the command: `gcloud artifacts docker images describe` with the `--show-provenance` flag.

The key distinction is that Cloud Build can generate provenance aligned with the SLSA v1.0 spec when you use the option requestedVerifyOption with triggered builds.

# Securing Image Deployments: Key Limitations and Solutions

## Primary Attestation Limitations

The documentation identifies three critical constraints for Cloud Build attestations:

1. **Private Pool Restriction**: "Cloud Build does not generate attestations when you push images to Artifact Registry using an explicit `docker push` build step."

2. **Image Push Method**: To generate proper attestations, you must use the `images` field in your `docker build` step rather than explicit push commands. The guide notes this ensures proper attestation generation.

3. **Pipeline Separation**: "You must use separate build config files for your build pipeline and deployment pipeline" since attestations only generate after successful builds, before Binary Authorization validation.

## Enabling Attestations in Private Pools

For private pool builds, add the `requestedVerifyOption: VERIFIED` option to your build configuration. This directive enables attestation generation and provenance metadata collection for your images.

## Key Workaround

Use the `images` field within your `docker build` step instead of separate `docker push` commands. This approach ensures Cloud Build properly tracks and signs the image, making it attestation-eligible for Binary Authorization policies.

The multi-step requirement exists because Binary Authorization validates attestations *after* the build completes, so your deployment must occur in a separate pipeline stage.

# Cloud Build Triggers: Provenance and v2 Repository Settings

Based on the provided documentation, **there is no mention of `requestedVerifyOption` or provenance settings on triggers themselves**. The page focuses on trigger creation and management but does not discuss provenance configuration at the trigger level.

## v2 Repository (2nd Generation) Notes

The documentation does reference 2nd generation repositories:

> "Select **1st generation** or **2nd generation** as your source. You can only connect repositories from GitHub and GitHub Enterprise when selecting **2nd generation** as your source."

However, **no provenance-specific settings are detailed for 2nd generation connections**. The page emphasizes that provenance configuration would be handled in the build config file, not trigger settings themselves.

## Key Limitation

The document states that trigger configuration handles events, source selection, and build execution parameters, but **build-level security features like provenance attestation appear to be managed through the build config file rather than trigger-specific options**.

For provenance settings, you would likely need to consult the build configuration schema documentation rather than trigger management guidance.

## Build Provenance

# Cosign Signing in Cloud Build: Key Details

## Image Pushing with Signing

The documentation explicitly uses **skopeo** for pushing images while preserving digests:

> "This pushes the image to google artifact registry. We are using skopeo with the [--preserve-digests] flag. This will give the image hash we'd expect"

This approach ensures deterministic container hashes remain consistent when transferred to registries.

## Identity Token Mechanism

The process leverages GCP service account OIDC tokens through **Fulcio** for keyless signing:

> "This step will use the service accounts OIDC token sign using Fulcio for the syft sbom"

The service account email (`cosign-svc-account@$PROJECT_ID.iam.gserviceaccount.com`) gets embedded in the signing certificate's Subject Alternative Name, with the OIDC issuer (`https://accounts.google.com`) recorded via OID `1.3.6.1.4.1.57264.1.1`.

## Provenance Alternative

Rather than using Cloud Build's standard `images` field, this approach creates **in-toto attestations** with custom predicates containing build metadata:

```json
{ "projectid": "$PROJECT_ID", "buildid": "$BUILD_ID", "commitsha": "$COMMIT_SHA" }
```

These attestations are signed separately and stored in the registry, providing verifiable supply chain artifacts independent of native provenance mechanisms.

# Cloud Build Attestation Configuration Details

## Private Pool Attestations

By default, Cloud Build does not generate Binary Authorization attestations for private pool builds. To enable them, add `requestedVerifyOption: VERIFIED` to your build configuration file. This option enables both attestation generation and provenance metadata for your image.

## Docker Push Limitation

"Cloud Build does not generate attestations when you push images to Artifact Registry using an explicit `docker push` build step."

Instead, you must use the `images` field in your `docker build` step to store images in Artifact Registry for attestation generation to work properly.

## Key Requirement

The documentation emphasizes that separate build config files are necessary for build and deployment pipelines, since attestations are only produced after the build pipeline completes successfully. Binary Authorization then validates these attestations before deployment proceeds.

## Configuration Example

```yaml
steps:
  - name: 'gcr.io/cloud-builders/docker'
    args: ['build', '-t', 'IMAGE_URL', '.']
images:
  - 'IMAGE_URL'
options:
  requestedVerifyOption: VERIFIED
```

# Cloud Build Provenance: Generation and Validation

## Overview

Cloud Build generates **Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts (SLSA) level 3 assurance** metadata that documents build integrity. This provenance includes image digests, source locations, build arguments, and execution duration—enabling verification that artifacts originate from trusted sources.

## Key Prerequisites

Before beginning, you must:
- Enable Cloud Build, Container Analysis, and Artifact Registry APIs
- Install and update the Google Cloud SDK
- Prepare source code and an Artifact Registry repository

## Generation Requirements

**Critical configuration elements:**

1. **Images Field**: Configure the `images` field in your build config to push to Artifact Registry. Cloud Build cannot generate provenance when you use explicit `docker push` steps.

2. **Verification Option**: Add to your build configuration's `options` section:
   ```
   requestedVerifyOption: VERIFIED
   ```
   This setting mandates provenance generation and marks builds successful only when provenance is present.

3. **Trigger Requirement**: To obtain both SLSA v1.0 and v0.1 provenance, you must use build triggers. Manual CLI submissions provide only SLSA v0.1 provenance.

## Important Limitations

- Provenance generation applies exclusively to artifacts in Artifact Registry
- Repository attachments aren't subject to cleanup policies; they're deleted when their associated image is removed

## BuildType Schema

As part of SLSA v1.0 support, Cloud Build provides `buildType` details describing the parameterized template, values recorded, and their sources. Reference the [Cloud Build buildType v1](https://cloud.google.com/build/gcb-buildtypes/google-worker/v1) specification.

## Viewing Provenance

**Console Access**: The Security insights side panel displays provenance details with viewable snippets.

**CLI Command**:
```
gcloud artifacts docker images describe [IMAGE]@sha256:[HASH] \
    --show-provenance --format=[FORMAT]
```

Output includes build metadata, resolved dependencies, builder identification, and cryptographic signatures.

## Validation Methods

**SLSA Verifier Tool**: Install version 2.1+ and run:
```
slsa-verifier verify-image "[IMAGE]" \
    --provenance-path provenance.json \
    --source-uri [SOURCE] \
    --builder-id=[BUILDER_ID]
```

**Manual Validation**: Decode base64-encoded payload and signature from the envelope, retrieve the public key from Cloud KMS, transform to DSSE PAE format, and verify using OpenSSL.

## Non-Container Artifacts

Cloud Build generates SLSA provenance for standalone Go, Java (Maven), Python, and Node.js applications uploaded to Artifact Registry. Retrieve metadata via direct Container Analysis API calls—this provenance isn't accessible through the console or gcloud CLI.

content.text:
I now have comprehensive research across all five questions. Let me compile the findings.

---

## Google Cloud Build SLSA Provenance: Research Findings

### Question 1: Does `requestedVerifyOption: VERIFIED` in build config options generate provenance for trigger-invoked builds on private pools?

**Yes, but it requires explicit opt-in.** By default, Cloud Build does NOT generate Binary Authorization attestations for private pool builds. The `requestedVerifyOption: VERIFIED` opt-in is the mechanism to enable it.

The key sequence:
- Default private pool behavior: attestations and provenance are skipped
- Adding `requestedVerifyOption: VERIFIED` to the `options` block in the build config activates both attestation generation AND provenance metadata collection for private pool builds
- Once set, builds fail with a non-SUCCESS status if provenance cannot be generated (the option acts as a gate, not just a request)

From the [Securing image deployments to Cloud Run and GKE doc](https://docs.cloud.google.com/build/docs/securing-builds/secure-deployments-to-run-gke):

> "By default, Cloud Build does not generate Binary Authorization attestations for builds in private pools. To generate attestations, add the `requestedVerifyOption: VERIFIED` option to your build configuration file."

The minimum working config for a private pool with provenance:

```yaml
options:
  requestedVerifyOption: VERIFIED
steps:
  - name: 'gcr.io/cloud-builders/docker'
    args: ['build', '-t', 'REGION-docker.pkg.dev/PROJECT/REPO/IMAGE:TAG', '.']
images:
  - 'REGION-docker.pkg.dev/PROJECT/REPO/IMAGE:TAG'
```

---

### Question 2: Does the top-level `images:` field need to be populated? What about crane/skopeo pushes?

**Yes, the `images:` field is required, and crane/skopeo pushes do NOT generate provenance.**

This is documented explicitly and unambiguously. From the [Generate and validate build provenance doc](https://docs.cloud.google.com/build/docs/securing-builds/generate-validate-build-provenance):

> "Cloud Build can't generate provenance if you push your image to Artifact Registry using an explicit `docker push` step."

The same restriction applies to any explicit push mechanism, including crane and skopeo — these are not Cloud Build's native push path. Provenance is generated by the Cloud Build platform itself as a post-build action when the `images:` field is populated. The platform-side generation is what makes it SLSA Level 3 (non-forgeable, service-generated).

**What the `images:` field does:** It signals to Cloud Build to handle the final push to Artifact Registry internally after the build steps complete. This internal push is what triggers provenance generation. Bypassing it with any tool (docker push, crane, skopeo) breaks the provenance chain.

**Implication for your use case:** If your build steps use crane or skopeo to push to Artifact Registry, provenance will not be generated even with `requestedVerifyOption: VERIFIED` set. You would need to restructure so the image is pushed via the `images:` field, not an explicit push step.

---

### Question 3: Alternative provenance mechanisms

Several alternatives exist:

**A. Cosign keyless signing via Cloud Build identity tokens** (documented at [salrashid123/cosign_bazel_cloud_build](https://github.com/salrashid123/cosign_bazel_cloud_build)):
- Cloud Build can issue Google-signed OIDC identity tokens (GA since August 2023)
- You can use cosign within a build step to sign the image using the Fulcio keyless mechanism
- The service account email gets embedded in the signing certificate's SAN
- This creates verifiable attestations stored in the registry as OCI artifacts
- This is NOT the same as Cloud Build's native SLSA provenance, but it is a valid alternative for supply chain verification
- The example uses skopeo with `--preserve-digests` for pushing, then signs separately

**B. Manual Artifact Analysis API attestations:**
- The [aactl tool](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/aactl) can ingest SLSA provenance and sigstore attestations into Artifact Analysis
- This supports importing provenance generated by SLSA GitHub Generator or other builders
- Useful if you need to represent builds from external systems in GCP's attestation store

**C. SLSA v0.1 provenance without triggers:**
- Manual `gcloud builds submit` invocations generate SLSA v0.1 provenance (without the trigger requirement)
- v0.1 provenance is less complete than v1.0 but is still recorded

**D. No trigger-level `--requested-verify-option` flag:**
- There is no documented `--requested-verify-option` flag for `gcloud builds triggers create` or trigger management
- The option lives exclusively in the build config file's `options` block
- Trigger creation and provenance generation are separate concerns

---

### Question 4: Private pool and provenance interaction / limitations

**Confirmed limitations:**

1. **Default behavior is no provenance** — private pool builds produce neither attestations nor provenance unless explicitly configured
2. **`requestedVerifyOption: VERIFIED` re-enables it** — the option brings private pools to feature parity with default pools for attestation purposes
3. **Artifact Registry is still required** — provenance only attaches to images stored in Artifact Registry (not Container Registry, not other registries)
4. **Network constraints are orthogonal** — private pool VPC peering restrictions (e.g., no transitive peering to managed services like GKE or Cloud SQL) do not affect provenance generation itself, since provenance is handled by the Cloud Build control plane, not the worker VM's network
5. **No documented hard limitation making private pool provenance impossible** — the documentation presents it as supported with explicit opt-in

The interaction between private pools and provenance generation appears to be: private pools run in your VPC for network access reasons, but the provenance signing infrastructure (Cloud KMS, Artifact Analysis, the signing service) is Google-managed and operates out-of-band from the build worker's network path.

---

### Question 5: CB v2 (GitLab) connection type and provenance availability

**The documentation does not document a specific limitation for GitLab v2 connections**, but there is an important nuance found in the trigger creation documentation:

From [Create and manage build triggers](https://docs.cloud.google.com/build/docs/automating-builds/create-manage-triggers):

> "You can only connect repositories from GitHub and GitHub Enterprise when selecting 2nd generation as your source."

This is potentially significant: the "2nd generation" UI trigger creation path appears limited to GitHub/GitHub Enterprise. GitLab connections through Cloud Build Repositories (2nd gen) use a different pathway (Developer Connect / Cloud Build Repositories API), not the standard trigger source selector.

**For SLSA v1.0 provenance specifically:**
- v1.0 provenance requires a triggered build (any trigger, not manual)
- Builds triggered by repository events (push, PR) generate v1.0
- The provenance `buildType` schema records which template and values were used

**Practical implication:** If your GitLab connection is a v2/2nd-gen repository connection going through Developer Connect, the trigger still fires on repository events and should produce v1.0 provenance the same as other triggers — the connection type affects *source resolution*, not the provenance signing chain. However, this specific combination (GitLab 2nd gen + provenance) has **no explicit confirmation or denial** in current documentation, which is a gap.

---

### Summary Table

| Question | Finding | Confidence |
|----------|---------|------------|
| `requestedVerifyOption: VERIFIED` on private pools | Yes, works — explicit opt-in required | High (documented) |
| `images:` field required for provenance | Yes, mandatory. Explicit push steps (docker/crane/skopeo) block provenance | High (documented) |
| Alternative provenance mechanisms | Cosign + OIDC identity tokens (custom attestations); Artifact Analysis API import; SLSA v0.1 without trigger | Medium (documented, but different SLSA level) |
| Private pool provenance limitations | Default-off, opt-in with VERIFIED; network VPC constraints are orthogonal | High (documented) |
| v2/GitLab connection type effect | No documented restriction; but explicit v2+GitLab+provenance confirmation absent | Low-Medium (gap in docs) |

---

### Key Documentation URLs

- [Generate and validate build provenance](https://docs.cloud.google.com/build/docs/securing-builds/generate-validate-build-provenance) — primary reference
- [Securing image deployments to Cloud Run and GKE](https://docs.cloud.google.com/build/docs/securing-builds/secure-deployments-to-run-gke) — private pool attestation details
- [Build configuration file schema](https://docs.cloud.google.com/build/docs/build-config-file-schema) — `requestedVerifyOption` field definition
- [Private pools overview](https://docs.cloud.google.com/build/docs/private-pools/private-pools-overview)
- [Cloud Build release notes](https://docs.cloud.google.com/build/docs/release-notes) — SLSA v1.0 GA announced June 25, 2024
- [aactl: Artifact Analysis data import utility](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/aactl) — alternative provenance ingestion
- [cosign + Bazel + Cloud Build example](https://github.com/salrashid123/cosign_bazel_cloud_build) — cosign keyless signing alternative
- [Binary Authorization SLSA check](https://docs.cloud.google.com/binary-authorization/docs/cv-slsa-check) — enforcement mechanism
